### **Key Themes of Recent Updates**
*   **Refund & Billing Efficiency**: Significant work on automated refund eligibility (**[CRM-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-52)**), fraud detection (**[CRM-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-60)**), and chargeback integrations (**[CRM-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-14)**).
*   **Account Security & Recovery**: Multiple updates to the account recovery flow, including SMS-based recovery (**[CRM-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-27)**), backup codes (**[CRM-43](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-43)**), and automated lockout detection (**[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)**).
*   **Technical Escalations**: New workflows for escalating bugs to engineering (**[CRM-81](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-81)**) and proactive incident detection (**[CRM-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-97)**).

answer:
The CRM project currently contains **100 issues**, all of which are in the **To Do** status. These issues are organized into four major strategic initiatives aimed at improving customer experience and operational efficiency.

### **Project Summary**
*   **Total Issues**: 100
*   **Status Breakdown**: 100% To Do
*   **Key Initiatives**: Billing Dispute Resolution, Account Recovery Modernization, Refund Processing Efficiency, and Technical Issue Reporting.

---

### **1. Billing Dispute Resolution (CRM-1 to CRM-25)**
This initiative focuses on reducing the volume and resolution time of billing disputes through automation and better customer self-service.
*   **[CRM-1](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-1)**: Reduce Billing Dispute Resolution Time and Volume (Initiative Lead)
*   **[CRM-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-2)**: Implement Automated Billing Dispute Triage System
*   **[CRM-6](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-6)**: Create Customer Self-Service Billing Dispute Portal
*   **[CRM-11](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-11)**: Develop Proactive Billing Alert System for Unusual Charges
*   **[CRM-16](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-16)**: Streamline Internal Billing Adjustment Approval Workflow
*   **[CRM-21](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-21)**: Enhance Billing Statement Clarity and Transparency
*   *(Plus 19 supporting technical and design tasks)*

### **2. Account Recovery Modernization (CRM-26 to CRM-50)**
Aims to reduce the 20% of support volume caused by account access issues by implementing more secure and automated recovery methods.
*   **[CRM-26](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-26)**: Modernize Account Recovery Process to Reduce Support Volume (Initiative Lead)
*   **[CRM-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-27)**: Implement Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) for Account Recovery
*   **[CRM-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-31)**: Build Automated Identity Verification Pipeline
*   **[CRM-36](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-36)**: Create Secure "Trusted Contact" Recovery Method
*   **[CRM-41](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-41)**: Implement Behavioral Biometrics for Risk-Based Recovery
*   **[CRM-46](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-46)**: Develop Account Recovery Health Dashboard
*   *(Plus 19 supporting technical and design tasks)*

### **3. Refund Processing Efficiency (CRM-51 to CRM-75)**
Focuses on automating refund eligibility and improving the tracking of physical returns to reduce manual agent workload.
*   **[CRM-51](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-51)**: Improve Refund Processing Efficiency and Customer Experience (Initiative Lead)
*   **[CRM-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-52)**: Implement Instant Refund Eligibility Engine for Low-Risk Orders
*   **[CRM-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-56)**: Create Customer-Facing Refund Status Tracking Portal
*   **[CRM-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-60)**: Develop Refund Fraud Detection Model with Machine Learning
*   **[CRM-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-64)**: Implement Proactive Refund Offers for Delayed Orders
*   **[CRM-68](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-68)**: Streamline Return Label Generation and Tracking
*   **[CRM-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-72)**: Build Refund Policy Exception Workflow
*   *(Plus 18 supporting technical and design tasks)*

### **4. App Crash & Technical Issue Reporting (CRM-76 to CRM-100)**
Aims to bridge the gap between support and engineering by automating the capture of technical context during app failures.
*   **[CRM-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-76)**: Enhance App Crash and Technical Issue Reporting Pipeline (Initiative Lead)

answer:
Based on an analysis of the **CRM (Customer Support)** project issues, several systemic gaps in the current workflow have been identified. These gaps are currently being addressed through a series of modernization and automation initiatives:

### 1. High Manual Burden in Account Recovery
A significant portion of support volume appears to stem from manual account recovery and security lockouts.
*   **Gap**: Lack of self-service and automated security protocols.
*   **Evidence**: Issues like **[CRM-26](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-26)** (Modernize Account Recovery to Reduce Volume) and **[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)** (Automated Account Lockout Detection) indicate that agents are currently the primary bottleneck for routine security tasks.
*   **Impact**: Increased ticket volume and slower resolution for users locked out of their accounts.

### 2. Inefficient Billing & Refund Workflows
The current process for handling disputes and refunds is largely manual and lacks integration with payment providers.
*   **Gap**: Absence of automated eligibility checks and direct API integrations.
*   **Evidence**: **[CRM-51](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-51)** (Improve Refund Processing Efficiency) and **[CRM-54](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-54)** (Integrate with Stripe/PayPal APIs) suggest that agents are likely jumping between systems to process financial transactions.
*   **Impact**: High "Time to Resolve" for billing issues and increased risk of human error in refund processing.

### 3. Technical Context Deficit
Agents often lack the necessary technical data to troubleshoot issues on the first contact.
*   **Gap**: No automated collection of customer environment data or crash logs.
*   **Evidence**: **[CRM-89](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-89)** (Customer Technical Environment Collection) and **[CRM-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-76)** (Enhance App Crash Reporting) highlight a need for better data at the point of ticket creation.
*   **Impact**: Excessive "back-and-forth" communication with customers to gather basic technical details (OS, app version, etc.).

### 4. Reactive vs. Proactive Support
The workflow is currently triggered almost exclusively by customer complaints rather than system alerts.
*   **Gap**: Lack of proactive monitoring for customer-facing failures (e.g., shipping delays, technical outages).
*   **Evidence**: **[CRM-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-64)** (Proactive Refund Offers for Delayed Shipments) and **[CRM-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-97)** (Technical Issue Pattern Detection) are being built to bridge this gap.
*   **Impact**: Support is always "behind the curve," dealing with frustrated customers who have already experienced a failure.

### 5. Visibility and SLA Tracking Gaps
There is a systemic lack of real-time visibility into performance metrics and escalation paths.
*   **Gap**: Missing real-time SLA indicators and centralized escalation dashboards.
*   **Evidence**: **[CRM-22](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-22)** (Dispute Resolution SLA Tracking) and **[CRM-84](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-84)** (Escalation Tracking Dashboard) indicate these metrics were previously unmonitored or siloed.
*   **Impact**: Difficulty in identifying bottlenecks in real-time and inconsistent response times for complex cases.

### Summary of Patterns
| Pattern | Systemic Gap | Primary Solution in Progress |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Volume Bloat** | Manual Account Recovery | **[CRM-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-27)** (SMS Recovery) |
| **Process Friction** | Manual Billing/Refunds | **[CRM-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-52)** (Eligibility Engine) |
| **Information Gap** | Missing Technical Context | **[CRM-91](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-91)** (Auto-detection Library) |
| **Operational Blindness** | Lack of SLA/Escalation Tracking | **[CRM-24](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-24)** (Real-time SLA Indicators) |

answer:
Based on an analysis of the issues in the **BUGS** project, the mobile application is currently facing several critical technical challenges that directly impact stability, performance, and security.

The underlying technical problems can be categorized into six key areas:

### 1. Memory Management & Stability
*   **User Symptom**: Frequent app crashes, particularly when viewing attachments.
*   **Technical Root Cause**: The iOS app attempts to load large attachments (15MB+) entirely into memory before rendering a preview. This triggers `EXC_RESOURCE` memory exhaustion crashes, especially on devices with 2GB RAM like the iPhone 8 or SE.
*   **Key Issue**: **[BUGS-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-52)** (Accounts for 23% of all iOS crashes).

### 2. UI Thread Blocking (Performance Jank)
*   **User Symptom**: Noticeable stuttering and frame drops (15-20fps) while scrolling through issue lists.
*   **Technical Root Cause**: Several expensive operations are being performed on the main thread during scrolling:
    *   Rich text (Markdown) rendering.
    *   Synchronous loading of avatar images.
    *   Lack of caching for complex cell height calculations.
*   **Key Issues**: **[BUGS-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-72)**, **[BUGS-75](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-75)**.

### 3. Network Resilience & Offline Support
*   **User Symptom**: The app "hangs" indefinitely on poor connections; users cannot work without an active internet connection.
*   **Technical Root Cause**: 
    *   **Silent Failures**: On Android, network timeouts (OkHttp) are not handled at the UI layer, leaving loading spinners active indefinitely.
    *   **Lack of Persistence**: There is no local queue (Room/CoreData) to store "Pending" issues, meaning all creation logic requires a synchronous network response.
*   **Key Issues**: **[BUGS-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-56)**, **[BUGS-68](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-68)**, **[BUGS-69](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-69)**.

### 4. Background Resource Inefficiency
*   **User Symptom**: Excessive battery drain (15-20% daily) even when the app is not in use.
*   **Technical Root Cause**: 
    *   Background sync runs on a fixed 5-minute interval regardless of data changes.
    *   Legacy code is still requesting location services and maintaining active WebSockets in the background.
    *   Lack of battery-aware scheduling (e.g., `WorkManager` constraints or `BGAppRefreshTask`).
*   **Key Issues**: **[BUGS-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-60)**, **[BUGS-61](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-61)**, **[BUGS-62](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-62)**.

### 5. Navigation & Deep Linking Logic
*   **User Symptom**: Tapping a notification opens the wrong issue or the previous app state.
*   **Technical Root Cause**: On Android, the deep link handler executes before the navigation stack is fully initialized. The app restores its previous state and "drops" the new navigation intent from the notification.
*   **Key Issue**: **[BUGS-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-64)**.

### 6. Security & Session Synchronization
*   **User Symptom**: Users remain logged in on mobile after changing their password on the web; MFA enrollment crashes.
*   **Technical Root Cause**: 
    *   **Sync Lag**: Mobile apps only check session validity on foregrounding; there is no push-based revocation channel (FCM/APNs) to force logout.
    *   **API Incompatibility**: MFA crashes on Android 12+ due to strict `BiometricPrompt` requirements and a lack of version-specific abstraction layers.
*   **Key Issues**: **[BUGS-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-14)**, **[BUGS-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-18)**, **[BUGS-20](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-20)**.

### Summary of Impact
The **Mobile Application Stability and Performance Initiative ([BUGS-51](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-51))** has been created to address these issues, as current crash rates (2.3% iOS / 3.8% Android) are significantly above the 1% target.

answer:
Based on an analysis of the issues in the **BUGS** project, the common root causes behind API-related bugs and technical debt fall into several distinct categories:

### 1. Inconsistent Data Standards (Timezones & Dates)
The most prevalent technical issue is the lack of a unified standard for date and time representation across different endpoints.
*   **Root Cause:** Ad-hoc implementation where some endpoints use UTC, others use server local time, and some omit timezone indicators entirely.
*   **Impact:** This causes integration bugs for clients in different timezones and requires significant normalization effort.
*   **Key Issues:** **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)** (Inconsistent timezone handling), **[BUGS-100](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-100)** (Input normalization), and **[BUGS-98](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-98)** (Audit of response consistency).

### 2. Protocol-Level Logic Errors (Rate Limiting)
There is a specific recurring issue with how the API infrastructure handles browser-based security protocols.
*   **Root Cause:** The rate limiter incorrectly counts CORS `OPTIONS` preflight requests against the user's quota.
*   **Impact:** Users of Single Page Applications (SPAs) hit rate limits twice as fast as expected because every "real" request is preceded by a preflight request that consumes quota.
*   **Key Issue:** **[BUGS-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-85)** (Rate limiter counting preflights).

### 3. Structural Inconsistency (API Design Debt)
The "v1" API suffers from significant design drift and lack of a strict RESTful specification.
*   **Root Cause:** Inconsistent naming conventions (mixing singular and plural resources like `/issue` vs `/projects`) and varying methods for passing IDs (path vs. query parameters).
*   **Impact:** High developer friction and confusion, leading to increased support tickets.
*   **Key Issue:** **[BUGS-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-77)** (Inconsistent REST resource structure).

### 4. Platform-Specific Security Changes (Mobile APIs)
For mobile-facing APIs, bugs are frequently triggered by external OS updates.
*   **Root Cause:** Stricter security requirements in newer OS versions (e.g., Android 12+) and library updates (AndroidX Biometric) that break existing callback patterns.
*   **Impact:** Critical application crashes during sensitive flows like MFA enrollment.
*   **Key Issues:** **[BUGS-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-18)** (MFA crashes on Android 12+) and **[BUGS-20](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-20)** (Version-specific biometric handling).

### 5. Documentation & Discovery Gaps
A major "soft" root cause for API bugs is the lack of accurate, interactive documentation.
*   **Root Cause:** Relying on static Markdown files that are not synced with the actual implementation.
*   **Impact:** Developers use "trial-and-error" to understand the API, leading to incorrect implementations and reported "bugs" that are actually misunderstandings of the undocumented behavior.
*   **Key Issues:** **[BUGS-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-93)** (Interactive portal needs) and **[BUGS-94](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-94)** (OpenAPI documentation setup).

### Summary of Key Issues
| Key | Summary | Primary Root Cause |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)** | Fix API returning inconsistent timezone handling | Lack of unified date/time standard |
| **[BUGS-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-85)** | Fix rate limiter incorrectly counting OPTIONS preflight | Logic error in quota calculation |
| **[BUGS-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-77)** | Design and implement consistent REST API v2 | Ad-hoc design and technical debt |
| **[BUGS-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-18)** | MFA enrollment flow crashes on Android 12+ | OS-level API security changes |

These issues are currently being consolidated under the **API v2 Modernization Initiative ([BUGS-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-76))** to address these root causes systematically.

args.jql: project = "BUGS" AND issuetype = Bug AND (summary ~ "API" OR description ~ "API")

answer:
The analysis of the **BUGS** project reveals a cluster of issues related to inconsistent datetime and timezone handling.

### Identified Issues
1. **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)**: **Fix API returning inconsistent timezone handling for date fields**
   - This is the primary bug report detailing how different endpoints return dates in varying formats: some in UTC (correct), some in server local time (incorrect), and some without any timezone indicator at all (ambiguous).
2. **[BUGS-98](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-98)**: **Audit all API responses for datetime field consistency**
   - An initiative to map out all inconsistent fields and identify database columns that are incorrectly storing local time instead of UTC.
3. **[BUGS-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-99)**: **Implement datetime serializer with ISO 8601 UTC output**
   - A task to create a centralized JSON serializer to force all API outputs to follow the ISO 8601 standard with a `Z` (UTC) suffix.
4. **[BUGS-100](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-100)**: **Add input datetime normalization with deprecation warnings**
   - A task to ensure all incoming data (regardless of format) is normalized to UTC internally upon receipt.

### Common Technical Root Cause
The common technical root cause is the **absence of a unified datetime standard across the application's layers (API, Logic, and Database)**. 

Specifically, the system suffers from:
*   **Non-Standardized Storage**: Some database columns were configured to store "server time" rather than UTC, leading to data corruption when the server environment or timezone settings change.
*   **Decentralized Serialization**: Individual endpoints or services are likely using default or varied serialization logic rather than a single, global configuration, resulting in the mix of ISO 8601, raw strings, and local time formats.
*   **Ambiguous Input Parsing**: The system lacks a strict normalization layer at the API gateway/input level, allowing inconsistent formats to propagate into the internal logic.

args.jql: project = "BUGS" AND (text ~ "datetime" OR text ~ "timezone" OR text ~ "time zone")

answer:
Based on the high-priority issues in the **CRM** project, the main security and fraud-related concerns center around account security, refund fraud detection, and dispute management.

### 1. Refund Fraud Detection
There is a significant concern regarding refund fraud, with an estimated 4% fraud rate and less than half of it being caught.
*   **[CRM-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-60)**: **Develop Refund Fraud Detection Model with Machine Learning**
    *   **Concern**: Patterns include "item-not-received" claims after delivery, serial refunders across multiple accounts, and the return of counterfeit items.
    *   **Action**: Implementing an ML model to score refund requests and routing high-risk cases (>75 score) to a specialist queue with a 48-hour hold.

### 2. Account Takeover (ATO) & Identity Verification
The project has identified a trend of account takeovers (12 incidents last quarter) where attackers change email addresses after phishing passwords.
*   **[CRM-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-31)**: **Create Identity Verification Workflow for High-Risk Account Changes**
    *   **Concern**: Unauthorized sensitive account changes (email/password).
    *   **Action**: Requiring re-authentication and confirmation from both old and new emails for changes, or government ID uploads if the old email is inaccessible.
*   **[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)**: **Develop Automated Account Lockout Detection**
    *   **Concern**: Legitimate users are frequently locked out due to failed logins, creating a support burden and potential security frustration.
    *   **Action**: Implementing clearer messaging and self-service unlocks for known devices.

### 3. Secure Account Recovery
Current recovery processes are manual and slow, which can lead to users being locked out of their accounts for days.
*   **[CRM-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-27)**: **Implement SMS-Based Account Recovery**
    *   **Concern**: Users who lose email access have no self-service way to recover accounts.
    *   **Action**: Adding SMS-based recovery with rate-limiting (3 attempts/hour) and 10-minute code expiration to balance security and accessibility.

### 4. Dispute & Evidence Management
Manual processes for handling billing disputes are leading to inconsistent data and slow resolution times, which can be exploited.
*   **[CRM-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-2)**: **Automated Transaction Evidence Retrieval**
    *   **Concern**: Agents spend too much time manually pulling logs, which delays fraud/dispute investigations.
    *   **Action**: Automating the compilation of payment gateway responses, IP geolocation, and transaction history into an "evidence packet."
*   **[CRM-6](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-6)**: **Self-Service Dispute Submission Portal**
    *   **Concern**: Inconsistent information capture from email/chat-based disputes.
    *   **Action**: Creating a structured portal to automatically categorize and validate dispute claims at the source.

answer:
Based on an examination of the issues in the **BUGS** project, developer confusion regarding the API and its documentation stems from several key areas of inconsistency and technical debt:

### 1. Inconsistent Data Formats & Timezones
*   **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)**: Developers are confused by inconsistent timezone handling. Some endpoints return UTC, others return server time without indicators, and some return ambiguous date-only strings. This leads to bugs in third-party integrations that rely on precise timing.

### 2. Outdated and Static Documentation
*   **[BUGS-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-93)**: The current documentation is a static Markdown file that is frequently out of sync with the actual API implementation. This forces developers to use "trial-and-error" or open support tickets to understand how endpoints actually behave.
*   **[BUGS-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-76)**: General "poor documentation" is cited as a primary hurdle for third-party integrations, contributing to a high volume of support tickets.

### 3. Structural Inconsistencies (REST Design)
*   **[BUGS-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-77)**: The API lacks a consistent resource structure. Developers struggle with:
    *   **Naming**: Mixing singular and plural endpoints (e.g., `/issue/{id}` vs `/projects/{id}`).
    *   **Parameters**: Inconsistent use of path parameters vs. query parameters for IDs.
    *   **Error Responses**: Error formats vary wildly between endpoints, making it difficult to write consistent error-handling logic.

### 4. Unexpected Rate Limiting Behavior
*   **[BUGS-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-85)**: The rate limiter incorrectly counts CORS `OPTIONS` preflight requests against a user's quota. This causes developers to hit limits twice as fast as expected, especially in Single Page Applications (SPAs). Additionally, the absence of rate limit headers makes it impossible for developers to programmatically manage their request pacing.

### 5. Latency in System State (Search Indexing)
*   **[BUGS-39](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-39)**: A 30-60 second delay in search indexing causes "freshness" confusion. Developers/users create an issue and immediately search for it; when it doesn't appear, they assume the API call failed and often create duplicate records.

### 6. Missing Modern Features
*   **[BUGS-81](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-81)** & **[BUGS-89](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-89)**: The lack of webhooks and GraphQL forces developers into inefficient polling patterns and "N+1" query problems (making 4+ calls for a single view), which complicates their integration architecture.

### Summary of Root Causes
| Category | Primary Issue | Impact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reliability** | Inconsistent Timezones | Data integrity issues in integrations. |
| **Usability** | Static/Outdated Docs | High support volume; trial-and-error development. |
| **Predictability** | Rate Limiting Bugs | Unexpected "429 Too Many Requests" errors. |
| **Consistency** | Mixed REST Patterns | Difficult to build reusable API client libraries. |

answer:
Based on an analysis of high-priority bugs in the **BUGS** project, several recurring technical debt patterns have emerged. These patterns indicate underlying architectural weaknesses and "quick fixes" that are now impacting stability and performance.

### 1. Resource Management & Memory Leaks
A significant number of crashes and performance issues stem from improper handling of system resources.
*   **[BUGS-6](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-6)**: Memory leaks in the dashboard caused by orphaned subscriptions that aren't cleaned up on unmount.
*   **[BUGS-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-52)**: iOS crashes due to loading large attachments (15MB+) entirely into memory rather than using streaming or tiled previews.
*   **[BUGS-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-72)**: UI "jank" caused by expensive, uncached cell height calculations and synchronous image loading on the main thread.

### 2. Concurrency & State Synchronization
Race conditions and initialization timing issues are causing critical failures in authentication and navigation.
*   **[BUGS-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-2)**: A race condition in the OAuth flow where multiple browser tabs simultaneously attempt to refresh tokens, leading to unexpected logouts.
*   **[BUGS-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-64)**: Deep links on Android fail because the handler executes before the navigation stack is fully initialized, a classic "order of operations" debt.

### 3. Legacy Code & Outdated Implementations
Older code paths and rigid implementations are failing to meet modern requirements or library updates.
*   **[BUGS-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-60)**: Excessive battery drain caused by legacy code that requests location services it no longer uses and an inefficient 5-minute background sync.
*   **[BUGS-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-18)**: Biometric crashes occurring because the existing implementation didn't account for the stricter security requirements of newer AndroidX libraries.
*   **[BUGS-10](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-10)**: An overly rigid SAML parser that rejects valid enterprise attributes, indicating a "security-first" implementation that lacks the flexibility required for real-world integrations.

### 4. Inconsistent API & Data Standards
Lack of unified standards across the backend is creating friction for integrations and frontend logic.
*   **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)**: Inconsistent timezone handling where different endpoints return UTC, server time, or ambiguous strings.
*   **[BUGS-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-85)**: The rate limiter incorrectly penalizes CORS preflight `OPTIONS` requests, effectively halving the quota for SPA users.

### 5. Fragile Error Handling
The "happy path" was prioritized over resilient error states, leading to poor user experiences during network or input failures.
*   **[BUGS-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-56)**: Silent failures on Android where network timeouts aren't caught at the UI layer, leaving users stuck on infinite loading spinners.
*   **[BUGS-43](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-43)**: A search parser that lacks depth limiting, allowing complex queries to trigger stack overflows or infinite loops.

### Summary of Impact
| Pattern | Primary Impact | Key Issues |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Resource Debt** | Stability & Performance | **[BUGS-6](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-6)**, **[BUGS-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-52)**, **[BUGS-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-72)** |
| **Logic/State Debt** | Reliability | **[BUGS-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-2)**, **[BUGS-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-64)** |
| **Legacy/Rigidity** | Compatibility | **[BUGS-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-60)**, **[BUGS-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-18)**, **[BUGS-10](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-10)** |
| **Standardization** | Integration Friction | **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)**, **[BUGS-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-85)** |

args.jql: project = "BUGS" AND priority IN ("High", "Highest", "Critical", "Blocker") AND issuetype = "Bug" ORDER BY created DESC
key IN (BUGS-2, BUGS-6, BUGS-10, BUGS-18, BUGS-31, BUGS-43, BUGS-52, BUGS-56, BUGS-60, BUGS-64, BUGS-72, BUGS-85, BUGS-97)

answer:
Based on an analysis of the **CRM** project issues, the underlying causes of customer self-service gaps are primarily driven by a lack of automated workflows for common account and billing tasks. These gaps account for a significant portion of support volume (e.g., 34% for account access issues).

### Key Underlying Causes

1.  **Lack of Status Visibility**
    *   **Issue**: Customers have no way to track the progress of refund requests.
    *   **Impact**: This leads to "where is my refund" follow-up tickets, which consume 15% of agent capacity.
    *   **Reference**: **[CRM-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-56)**

2.  **Inflexible Account Recovery Options**
    *   **Issue**: Recovery is often limited to email. If a user loses access to their email, they must undergo a manual ID verification process taking 2-3 days.
    *   **Impact**: High volume of manual verification tickets that could be resolved via SMS or other secondary factors.
    *   **Reference**: **[CRM-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-27)**, **[CRM-26](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-26)**

3.  **Manual Lockout Management**
    *   **Issue**: Security protocols (like 5 failed login attempts) trigger a hard lockout with generic error messages, forcing users to email support.
    *   **Impact**: Most lockouts are legitimate users who forgot passwords, not attackers, creating unnecessary support overhead.
    *   **Reference**: **[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)**, **[CRM-37](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-37)**

4.  **Unstructured Dispute Processes**
    *   **Issue**: Billing disputes must be initiated via email or live chat rather than a structured web form.
    *   **Impact**: This results in inconsistent information capture and longer resolution times (currently averaging 7 days).
    *   **Reference**: **[CRM-6](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-6)**, **[CRM-1](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-1)**

### Summary of Gaps
| Gap Category | Primary Cause | Target Improvement |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Refunds** | No tracking portal | Reduce follow-up tickets by 15% |
| **Account Access** | Email-only recovery & hard lockouts | 50% reduction in recovery tickets |
| **Billing** | Manual/Unstructured intake | Reduce resolution time from 7 days to <48 hours |

args.jql: key IN (CRM-56, CRM-37, CRM-35, CRM-27, CRM-26, CRM-6, CRM-1)

answer:
Based on an analysis of the **BUGS** project issues, several critical design flaws in the current rate-limiting implementation are causing operational problems and security vulnerabilities:

### 1. Incorrect Quota Consumption (CORS Preflight Flaw)
The most immediate technical flaw is that the rate limiter incorrectly counts **CORS preflight `OPTIONS` requests** against a user's quota.
*   **Issues**: **[BUGS-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-85)**, **[BUGS-86](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-86)**
*   **Problem**: Every non-simple API call from a browser (common in SPAs) consumes **two quota units** (one for the `OPTIONS` preflight and one for the actual request).
*   **Impact**: Users hit their rate limits at 50% of the expected volume, leading to unexpected service interruptions for web-based integrations.

### 2. Lack of Developer Transparency
The system lacks standard communication protocols for rate limits, making it difficult for developers to build resilient integrations.
*   **Issues**: **[BUGS-87](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-87)**, **[BUGS-88](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-88)**
*   **Problem**: The API does not consistently return standard headers (`X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`). Additionally, there is no user-facing dashboard to monitor usage.
*   **Impact**: Developers cannot programmatically adjust their request velocity or anticipate when they will be throttled, resulting in a high volume of support tickets.

### 3. Vulnerability to Distributed Attacks (IP-only Scope)
The rate-limiting logic is too narrow, focusing solely on IP addresses rather than behavioral patterns.
*   **Issue**: **[BUGS-22](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-22)**
*   **Problem**: Rate limiting is applied strictly per IP. Attackers are bypassing this by using residential proxy networks to distribute credential stuffing attacks across thousands of IPs, with each IP staying just below the 10 requests/minute threshold.
*   **Impact**: A 400% increase in failed login attempts that the current system is unable to block, necessitating a shift toward behavioral analysis and bypass detection.

### 4. Architectural Technical Debt
Rate limiting is currently part of an aging API infrastructure that lacks modern standards.
*   **Issue**: **[BUGS-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-76)**
*   **Problem**: The API v1 architecture is described as having "significant technical debt" and "inconsistent naming," which includes the current rate-limiting implementation.
*   **Impact**: General developer friction and a need for a complete "Modernization Initiative" to implement API v2 with improved rate-limiting capabilities.

### Summary of Required Fixes:
1.  **Middleware Update**: Exclude `OPTIONS` requests from the rate-limiting middleware.
2.  **Header Implementation**: Standardize `X-RateLimit` headers across all responses.
3.  **Behavioral Logic**: Move beyond simple IP-based throttling to detect distributed attack patterns.
4.  **Observability**: Build a developer dashboard to show real-time usage vs. limits.

args.jql: issuetype = Bug AND (summary ~ "rate limit*" OR description ~ "rate limit*")
project = BUGS AND (text ~ "rate limit*" OR text ~ "throttling")

answer:
Based on the issue descriptions in the **CRM (Customer Support)** project, several critical monitoring and detection capabilities are currently missing or being implemented to address existing gaps. These can be categorized into technical, security, and operational domains:

### 1. Proactive Technical Issue Detection
*   **Ticket Pattern Recognition**: Currently, emerging technical issues are often detected through manual observation of ticket spikes before automated monitoring catches them. **[CRM-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-97)** highlights the need for real-time monitoring of ticket volume by category, app version, and device type to alert engineering proactively.
*   **Support-to-Engineering Escalation**: There is a lack of a formal, automated path for support-detected bugs. **[CRM-81](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-81)** notes that reproducible bugs are currently tracked inconsistently in spreadsheets or Slack, leading to lost information.
*   **Incident Integration**: **[CRM-95](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-95)** indicates a missing automated link between PagerDuty alerts and support incident workflows, which is necessary for mapping severity and auto-resolving issues.

### 2. Fraud and Security Monitoring
*   **Account Takeover (ATO) Detection**: **[CRM-47](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-47)** and **[CRM-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-31)** identify a lack of automated detection for fraudulent account recovery patterns, such as velocity spikes from specific IP ranges or sensitive account changes (e.g., email updates) following phishing attempts.
*   **Lockout Analytics**: There is no real-time monitoring for account lockout rates. **[CRM-38](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-38)** describes the need for a dashboard to alert when lockout rates spike more than 50% above the baseline.
*   **Refund Fraud**: **[CRM-55](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-55)** and **[CRM-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-60)** point to a missing machine learning-based detection system for refund fraud and automated monitoring of auto-approval rates.

### 3. Operational and SLA Monitoring
*   **Real-Time Chargeback Detection**: The team currently learns about chargebacks 3–5 days after they are filed. **[CRM-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-14)** identifies a missing integration with payment processor webhooks (Stripe/Braintree) to enable immediate action.
*   **Automated SLA Tracking**: Managers currently rely on manual spot-checks for dispute resolution timelines. **[CRM-22](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-22)** highlights that SLA breaches are often only caught when customers complain a second time.
*   **Shipping and Carrier Monitoring**: **[CRM-65](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-65)** mentions a lack of integrated shipping delay detection, which would allow support to be proactive rather than reactive to carrier issues.

### 4. Customer Visibility (Self-Service Detection)
*   **Public Status Page**: **[CRM-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-93)** notes that customers have no way to verify widespread outages (like API or payment processor downs) themselves, leading to floods of duplicate tickets.
*   **Refund Status Tracking**: Approximately 15% of support capacity is consumed by "where is my refund" inquiries because customers lack a self-service portal to track refund progress (**[CRM-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-56)**).

args.jql: project = CRM ORDER BY created DESC
project = CRM AND (description ~ "missing" OR description ~ "lack" OR description ~ "visibility" OR description ~ "notified" OR description ~ "alert")

answer:
Based on an analysis of the subtasks within the **CRM (Customer Support)** project, the team is focused on solving several key operational inefficiencies through automation, self-service, and improved data integration:

### 1. Manual Billing Dispute & Evidence Collection
Currently, agents appear to be manually gathering data for billing disputes, which is time-consuming and prone to error.
*   **Solution**: The team is building an automated evidence compilation pipeline.
*   **Key Subtasks**:
    *   **[CRM-3](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-3)**: Designing a standardized evidence packet schema and PDF template.
    *   **[CRM-4](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-4)**: Integrating the PaymentService API for automatic transaction retrieval.
    *   **[CRM-5](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-5)**: Creating background jobs to compile evidence automatically when a ticket is created.
    *   **[CRM-11](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-11)**: Implementing a clustering algorithm to handle complex subscription disputes.

### 2. High Support Volume for Account Recovery
A significant portion of support volume likely stems from users locked out of their accounts, requiring manual identity verification by agents.
*   **Solution**: Implementing self-service recovery tools and automated ID verification.
*   **Key Subtasks**:
    *   **[CRM-33](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-33)**: Integrating Jumio for automated government ID verification.
    *   **[CRM-37](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-37)**: Building a self-service account unlock flow.
    *   **[CRM-44](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-44)** & **[CRM-45](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-45)**: Developing a backup code system to allow users to recover accounts without agent help.

### 3. Inefficient Refund & Return Workflows
The current process for refunds and returns involves manual eligibility checks and manual label generation.
*   **Solution**: Automating the decision-making and execution of refunds and returns.
*   **Key Subtasks**:
    *   **[CRM-53](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-53)**: Building an eligibility rules engine to automate refund approvals.
    *   **[CRM-54](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-54)**: Integrating Stripe/PayPal APIs for programmatic refund processing.
    *   **[CRM-69](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-69)**: Automating return label generation via carrier APIs (EasyPost).
    *   **[CRM-71](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-71)**: Triggering auto-refunds immediately upon warehouse confirmation.

### 4. Reactive Technical Support & Lack of Context
Agents often lack the technical context (logs, device info) needed to solve app issues, leading to slow resolution times and excessive back-and-forth.
*   **Solution**: Proactive data collection and AI-assisted troubleshooting.
*   **Key Subtasks**:
    *   **[CRM-78](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-78)**: Linking Sentry crash reports directly to support tickets.
    *   **[CRM-86](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-86)**: Using OCR to automatically extract error messages from user screenshots.
    *   **[CRM-88](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-88)**: Building a UI to suggest troubleshooting guides to agents based on error patterns.
    *   **[CRM-90](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-90)**: Automating the detection of user environment data (OS, version, etc.).

### 5. Support-to-Engineering Communication Gaps
There is a lack of synchronization when support issues need to be escalated to engineering, leading to "black hole" tickets.
*   **Solution**: Bidirectional synchronization between Support and Engineering Jira projects.
*   **Key Subtasks**:
    *   **[CRM-82](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-82)**: Automating the creation of engineering bug tickets from support.
    *   **[CRM-83](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-83)**: Implementing bidirectional sync so agents see engineering updates in real-time.

Based on the issues tracked in the **BUGS** project, there are several critical inconsistencies and design flaws in the current API that the **REST API v2** initiative aims to resolve:

### 1. Resource Naming and Pluralization
*   **Issue**: **[BUGS-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-77)**
*   **Inconsistency**: API v1 currently mixes singular and plural nouns for resources (e.g., `/issue/{id}` vs. `/projects/{id}`).
*   **Fix**: Standardize all resource collections to use plural nouns (e.g., `/issues/{id}`).

### 2. Endpoint Parameter Patterns
*   **Issue**: **[BUGS-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-77)**
*   **Inconsistency**: Some endpoints use query parameters for identification (e.g., `/getProject?id={id}`), while others use path parameters (e.g., `/issue/{id}`).
*   **Fix**: Consistently use path parameters for resource identification and follow RESTful best practices.

### 3. Timezone and Date Handling
*   **Issue**: **[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)**
*   **Inconsistency**: Date fields are returned in three different, conflicting formats:
    *   **UTC with 'Z' suffix**: `2024-01-15T10:30:00Z` (Correct)
    *   **Server time without TZ**: `2024-01-15 05:30:00` (Incorrect/Ambiguous)
    *   **Date-only**: `2024-01-20` (Causes TZ shift bugs)
*   **Fix**: Standardize all date/time fields to UTC (ISO 8601) with the 'Z' suffix.

### 4. Error Response Standardization
*   **Issue**: **[BUGS-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-77)**
*   **Inconsistency**: Error responses vary wildly across different endpoints, making it difficult for developers to implement consistent error handling.
*   **Fix**: Implement a unified error object structure across all v2 endpoints.

### 5. Technical Debt and Feature Gaps
*   **Issue**: **[BUGS-76](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-76)**
*   **Inconsistency**: v1 lacks modern developer features like webhooks and consistent rate limiting.
*   **Fix**: The v2 initiative will introduce a GraphQL option, comprehensive webhooks, and a developer portal with interactive documentation to improve the overall Developer Experience (DX).

args.jql: project = BUGS AND text ~ "REST API v2" AND (text ~ "design" OR text ~ "inconsistency")

project = BUGS AND (text ~ "API" AND (text ~ "inconsistency" OR text ~ "design"))

Based on an analysis of the CRM project issues, the following fraud detection and prevention gaps have been identified in the current system:

### 1. Refund Fraud Detection Gaps
The system currently catches less than half of the estimated 4% refund fraud rate. Specific gaps include:
*   **Lack of Predictive Scoring**: There is no Machine Learning model to score refund requests based on risk factors like customer history, device fingerprinting, or claim type.
*   **Undetected Fraud Patterns**: The system fails to automatically flag "item-not-received" claims after confirmed delivery, serial refunders using multiple accounts, or the return of counterfeit items.
*   **Manual Review Bottlenecks**: Currently, **all** refunds require manual agent review regardless of risk level, which delays processing and prevents agents from focusing on high-risk cases.
*   **Relevant Issues**: **[CRM-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-60)**, **[CRM-63](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-63)**, **[CRM-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-52)**.

### 2. Account Takeover (ATO) & Recovery Vulnerabilities
Recent incidents (12 in the last quarter) highlight significant gaps in securing user accounts:
*   **Weak Verification for Sensitive Changes**: Email address changes currently lack multi-factor confirmation (e.g., requiring confirmation from both the old and new email addresses).
*   **Lack of IP/Velocity Intelligence**: The system does not automatically block or flag recovery attempts coming from known bad actor databases, TOR exit nodes, or high-velocity IP ranges.
*   **Inadequate Recovery Friction**: There is no automated identity verification workflow (like government ID uploads) for high-risk account recovery scenarios.
*   **Relevant Issues**: **[CRM-47](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-47)**, **[CRM-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-31)**.

### 3. Real-Time Monitoring & Response Gaps
*   **Delayed Chargeback Visibility**: The system currently learns about chargebacks 3–5 days after they are filed because it lacks real-time webhook integration with payment processors like Stripe and Braintree.
*   **Missing Anomaly Detection**: There is no dynamic baseline for "normal" system behavior, making it difficult to detect statistically significant deviations that indicate a coordinated attack or new fraud trend.
*   **Relevant Issues**: **[CRM-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-14)**, **[CRM-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-99)**.

### 4. Operational Gaps
*   **Lack of Specialist Tools**: Fraud specialists lack a dedicated review queue that provides "model explainability" (showing risk scores and top factors), which hinders their ability to make quick, informed decisions.
*   **Relevant Issues**: **[CRM-63](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-63)**.

args.jql: project = CRM AND (text ~ "fraud" OR text ~ "detection" OR text ~ "gap" OR text ~ "security") ORDER BY created DESC
key IN (CRM-63, CRM-60, CRM-52, CRM-47, CRM-35, CRM-31, CRM-14, CRM-99)

Based on the mobile issues in the **BUGS** project, there are several significant network error handling and connectivity problems currently being tracked:

### 1. Silent Failures & UI Freezes
*   **[BUGS-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-56)**: On poor connections (3G/spotty WiFi), the Android app hangs indefinitely. The network layer (OkHttp) times out after 60 seconds, but because the UI layer doesn't handle the exception, the loading spinner never disappears, forcing users to restart the app.

### 2. Lack of Robust Error Infrastructure
*   **[BUGS-57](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-57)**: There is currently no centralized mechanism to map network exceptions (timeouts, 404s, 500s) to user-friendly states. A task is open to implement a `NetworkExceptionHandler`.
*   **[BUGS-58](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-58)**: The app lacks automatic retry logic. Transient errors (like a momentary 503 or timeout) cause immediate failure rather than attempting an exponential backoff.

### 3. Offline Capability Gaps
*   **[BUGS-68](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-68)**: Users cannot create issues while offline. This is a critical issue for on-site workers in warehouses or data centers with no connectivity.
*   **[BUGS-59](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-59)**: There is no "Offline Mode" UI. The app does not currently cache issue lists or details for viewing when a connection is lost.

### 4. Sync & Session Management Issues
*   **[BUGS-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-60)**: The background sync process is inefficient, running every 5 minutes and waking the device radio even when no new data is available, leading to 15-20% daily battery drain.
*   **[BUGS-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-14)**: Security sessions are not invalidated in real-time on mobile. If a user changes their password on the web, the mobile app continues to use the old token for up to 24 hours because it lacks a persistent connection to receive revocation events.

args.jql: project = "BUGS" AND (text ~ "network" OR text ~ "connection" OR text ~ "timeout" OR text ~ "offline")
key IN (BUGS-56, BUGS-57, BUGS-58, BUGS-59, BUGS-60, BUGS-68, BUGS-14)

answer:
Based on an analysis of the 100 issues currently in the **To Do** status for the **BUGS** project, several recurring technical patterns and systemic problems emerge. These can be categorized into four primary areas:

### 1. Authentication & Security Fragility
A significant cluster of issues points to instability in the authentication layer, particularly regarding session management and modern security standards.
*   **Concurrency & State Management**: Race conditions in OAuth token refreshing (**[BUGS-2](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-2)**) and memory leaks in auth state subscriptions (**[BUGS-6](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-6)**) suggest the auth client logic is not handling asynchronous state changes robustly.
*   **SSO/SAML Parsing**: Multiple issues regarding SAML assertion failures (**[BUGS-10](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-10)**, **[BUGS-11](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-11)**) indicate a need for more permissive or updated parsing logic for enterprise customers.
*   **Mobile Auth Parity**: Session invalidation fails to propagate to mobile apps (**[BUGS-14](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-14)**), and MFA enrollment is crashing on newer Android versions (**[BUGS-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-18)**).

### 2. Search Infrastructure & Relevance Debt
The search system (Elasticsearch) is facing both performance bottlenecks and a lack of modern features.
*   **Version Obsolescence**: The system is running on an outdated Elasticsearch 6.8 cluster, with several tasks dedicated to migrating to 8.x (**[BUGS-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-27)**, **[BUGS-28](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-28)**).
*   **Pagination & Scale**: Deep pagination is currently broken (**[BUGS-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-31)**), requiring a shift to `search_after` cursors (**[BUGS-33](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-33)**).
*   **Query Parsing**: The search syntax parser is fragile, crashing on complex boolean queries (**[BUGS-43](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-43)**), which indicates a need for a more formal grammar or iterative parsing approach.

### 3. Mobile Resource & Network Management
The mobile applications (iOS and Android) suffer from recurring stability issues related to how they handle external data and background tasks.
*   **Memory & Large Assets**: The iOS app crashes when rendering issues with large attachments (**[BUGS-52](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-52)**), suggesting a lack of streaming or memory-limited image loading.
*   **Network Resilience**: Android requests fail silently on poor connections (**[BUGS-56](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-56)**), highlighting a lack of centralized error handling and exponential backoff strategies (**[BUGS-58](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-58)**).
*   **Background Efficiency**: Excessive battery drain from background sync (**[BUGS-60](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-60)**) and deep-linking bugs (**[BUGS-64](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-64)**) point to unoptimized background service handling.

### 4. API Consistency & Developer Experience (DX)
The REST API shows signs of "organic growth" leading to inconsistencies that affect both internal and external developers.
*   **Standardization Gaps**: Inconsistent timezone handling (**[BUGS-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-97)**) and varying error response formats (**[BUGS-79](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-79)**) are recurring complaints.
*   **Middleware Logic**: The rate limiter is incorrectly penalizing `OPTIONS` preflight requests (**[BUGS-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-85)**), which can break cross-origin web integrations.
*   **Modernization Needs**: There is a clear push to move toward GraphQL for complex data fetching (**[BUGS-89](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-89)**) and to implement webhooks for real-time events (**[BUGS-81](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-81)**).

### Summary of Technical Patterns
| Pattern | Recurring Symptom | Primary Impact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **State Synchronization** | Race conditions, memory leaks, sync lag | User session instability & data staleness |
| **Legacy Tech Debt** | ES 6.8, offset-based pagination | Search performance & scalability limits |
| **Resource Exhaustion** | Crashes on large files, battery drain | Poor mobile user experience & app reviews |
| **Schema Inconsistency** | Timezone mismatches, varied error formats | High friction for API consumers/integrations |

Based on an analysis of the **CRM (Customer Support)** project, the common causes of support ticket escalation problems stem from fragmented workflows, lack of automated tracking, and training gaps.

### **Common Causes of Escalation Problems**

1.  **Lack of Formal Engineering Escalation Paths**
    *   **Issue**: Support agents currently use inconsistent methods like spreadsheets, emails, or Slack to report reproducible bugs to engineering.
    *   **Impact**: Information is frequently lost, and there is no bidirectional sync to keep support updated on engineering progress.
    *   **Reference**: **[CRM-81](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-81)**

2.  **Inadequate SLA Monitoring and Alerting**
    *   **Issue**: There is a lack of automated tracking for internal SLAs (e.g., 48h for simple disputes, 5 days for complex ones).
    *   **Impact**: Managers rely on manual spot-checks, which often miss SLA breaches until a customer complains a second time.
    *   **Reference**: **[CRM-22](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-22)**, **[CRM-84](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-84)**

3.  **Agent Proficiency and Decision-Making Gaps**
    *   **Issue**: New agents struggle to distinguish between fraud and legitimate complaints, leading to uncertainty about when an escalation is necessary.
    *   **Impact**: This increases the time to proficiency (currently ~3 weeks) and leads to inconsistent escalation quality.
    *   **Reference**: **[CRM-18](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-18)**

4.  **Inefficient Handling of Multi-Transaction Issues**
    *   **Issue**: Workflows often treat related transactions (like recurring subscription errors) as separate tickets.
    *   **Impact**: This causes agents to create duplicate refunds or miss related transactions entirely, necessitating complex manual escalations to billing specialists.
    *   **Reference**: **[CRM-10](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-10)**

5.  **Manual Evidence Gathering and Tooling**
    *   **Issue**: High resolution times (averaging 7 days) are driven by manual processes for gathering evidence and managing chargebacks.
    *   **Impact**: Without integrated tooling and self-service forms, simple issues are unnecessarily escalated or delayed.
    *   **Reference**: **[CRM-1](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-1)**

### **Summary of Key Metrics & Goals**
*   **Target Resolution Time**: Reduce from 7 days to under 48 hours.
*   **Escalation Volume**: Addressing a 23% increase in complex subscription billing disputes.
*   **SLA Compliance**: Implementing visual indicators (Green/Yellow/Red) and auto-escalation to team leads when <4 hours remain on an SLA.

args.jql: project = CRM AND (summary ~ "escalation" OR description ~ "escalation" OR summary ~ "escalated" OR description ~ "escalated") ORDER BY created DESC
key IN (CRM-81, CRM-10, CRM-84, CRM-1, CRM-18, CRM-22)

Based on the current initiatives in the **CRM (Customer Support)** project, the team is addressing several critical account security vulnerabilities and operational gaps. The focus is primarily on preventing **Account Takeover (ATO)** and modernizing the **Account Recovery** infrastructure.

### **1. Account Takeover (ATO) & Phishing Mitigation**
The team is responding to a trend of unauthorized email changes following phishing attacks (12 incidents reported last quarter).
*   **High-Risk Verification**: **[CRM-31](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-31)** introduces a workflow requiring re-authentication and dual-email confirmation (old and new) for sensitive changes.
*   **Anomaly Detection**: **[CRM-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-99)** and **[CRM-47](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-47)** focus on detecting patterns such as recovery attempts from TOR nodes, VPNs, or known bad IP ranges, especially following external data breaches.

### **2. Fraudulent Account Recovery Prevention**
To combat sophisticated recovery fraud, the system is moving toward a risk-based approach.
*   **Risk Scoring**: **[CRM-40](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-40)** implements an algorithm (scoring 1-100) based on account age, transaction volume, and previous recovery history to dictate the level of verification required.
*   **Brute-Force Protection**: **[CRM-29](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-29)** specifically targets recovery code guessing by enforcing lockouts after 5 failed attempts within 30 minutes.

### **3. Modernizing Recovery Infrastructure**
The project is replacing legacy recovery methods with more secure and user-friendly alternatives:
*   **Multi-Channel Recovery**: Implementation of SMS-based recovery (**[CRM-27](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-27)**) and secure backup code generation (**[CRM-43](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-43)**, **[CRM-44](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-44)**).
*   **Knowledge-Based Verification**: **[CRM-41](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-41)** adds a layer of security questions for manual verification scenarios.

### **4. Operational Efficiency & User Experience**
A significant portion of the work addresses the "Support Bottleneck" caused by account lockouts.
*   **Self-Service Unlocks**: **[CRM-35](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-35)** and **[CRM-37](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-37)** enable legitimate users to unlock their accounts via email or SMS if the request originates from a known device, reducing manual support tickets.
*   **Agent Tooling**: **[CRM-39](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-39)** provides support agents with a dedicated toolkit to handle complex recovery cases more securely and efficiently.

### **5. Transparency & Monitoring**
*   **Security Activity Logs**: **[CRM-34](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-34)** will provide users with a log of security-related events (logins, password changes), allowing them to self-report suspicious activity.
*   **Lockout Analytics**: **[CRM-38](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-38)** provides a dashboard for administrators to monitor lockout trends and identify potential large-scale attacks in real-time.

Based on the issue descriptions in the **CRM** project, several significant troubleshooting automation gaps have been identified. These gaps currently lead to manual overhead, prolonged resolution times, and excessive back-and-forth communication between agents and customers.

### 1. Manual Technical Environment Collection
Currently, agents must manually request basic technical details (device, OS, app version, network type) for every technical ticket. This results in an average of **two additional back-and-forth messages** per issue.
*   **Gap**: Lack of automated client-side environment detection and standardized collection forms.
*   **Related Issue**: **[CRM-89](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-89)**: Implement Customer Technical Environment Collection Form.

### 2. Manual Error Code Lookup & Analysis
Agents are spending significant time manually identifying error codes from user-provided text or screenshots. There is no automated system to link these errors to known solutions.
*   **Gap**: Absence of an OCR pipeline to extract text from screenshots and a pattern-matching engine to identify known errors.
*   **Related Issues**: 
    *   **[CRM-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-85)**: Develop Automated Troubleshooting Guides Based on Error Messages.
    *   **[CRM-86](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-86)**: Implement OCR pipeline for error message extraction.

### 3. Missing In-App Crash Context
When the application crashes, users are forced to report the issue via email. This process fails to capture critical debugging data like stack traces, device state, or the user's journey leading up to the crash.
*   **Gap**: Lack of in-app crash reporting that automatically captures technical context and user actions.
*   **Related Issues**:
    *   **[CRM-77](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-77)**: Implement In-App Crash Report Submission with Automatic Context Capture.
    *   **[CRM-80](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-80)**: Implement user journey capture for crash context.

### 4. Disconnected Crash Monitoring & Support Triage
There is currently no automated link between crash monitoring tools (like Sentry) and the support ticketing system, leading to duplicate tickets for the same crash signatures.
*   **Gap**: Lack of integration between crash capture tools and support ticket creation for automated deduplication and linking.
*   **Related Issue**: **[CRM-78](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-78)**: Integrate Sentry crash capture with support ticket creation.

### 5. Lack of Proactive Troubleshooting Guidance
Even when an error is identified, there is no automated system to provide the user or agent with the specific troubleshooting steps required for that error.
*   **Gap**: No automated mapping between identified error patterns and step-by-step resolution guides.
*   **Related Issue**: **[CRM-85](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-85)**: Develop Automated Troubleshooting Guides Based on Error Messages.

Based on the descriptions in the **BUGS** project, the following database and storage issues are implied:

### 1. Scalability and Performance Degradation
As the bug database has grown to over 2 million issues, the current infrastructure is struggling to maintain performance.
*   **[BUGS-26](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-26)**: Reports that P95 search latency has spiked from 800ms to 4.2 seconds. This implies that the existing indexing or database structure is no longer sufficient for the current data volume, necessitating a move to Elasticsearch 8.x.
*   **[BUGS-49](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-49)**: Highlights the storage and processing challenges of backfilling embeddings for 2.3 million issues, noting an additional 1.2GB storage requirement for vector fields.

### 2. Data Consistency and Schema Issues
There are indications of technical debt regarding how data is stored and represented.
*   **[BUGS-98](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-98)**: Implies that datetime fields are inconsistent across the database. Specifically, it mentions the need to identify columns storing "local time" that require migration to a standardized format to ensure API consistency.

### 3. Local Persistence and Offline Synchronization
Several issues point to a lack of robust local storage for mobile clients, leading to problems with offline reliability.
*   **[BUGS-69](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-69)**: Describes the need for a persistent local queue (using Room/CoreData) to track pending issues, implying that current issue creation may fail or lose data if the connection is interrupted.
*   **[BUGS-59](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-59)**: Indicates that the application currently lacks effective caching for offline viewing, requiring a new implementation to store issue lists and details locally.

### 4. Large File Handling and Storage Management
The system appears to have limitations in how it handles non-text data.
*   **[BUGS-70](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-70)**: Implies that large attachments are currently failing or difficult to upload over poor connections. The solution involves implementing chunked uploads and a local storage cleanup mechanism to manage the device's "app documents" directory effectively.

Based on my analysis of the **BUGS** and **CRM** projects, there are currently no PagerDuty-related requests in the BUGS project. However, there are five active requests in the **CRM** project focused on proactive alerting, incident synchronization, and financial risk monitoring.

### **PagerDuty Integration Requests**

| Issue Key | Integration Point | Purpose / "Why" |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **[CRM-100](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-100)** | **Alerting Integration** | To notify engineering via PagerDuty when ticket volume for critical categories exceeds **5x the baseline** (P1 patterns). |
| **[CRM-97](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-97)** | **Proactive Engineering Alerts** | To catch emerging issues through **ticket volume spikes** before standard monitoring detects them. Engineering on-call receives alerts for critical patterns. |
| **[CRM-95](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-95)** | **Incident Sync & Escalation** | To **auto-create incidents** from PagerDuty alerts tagged "customer-facing" and allow manual escalation from support. Includes severity mapping and auto-resolution. |
| **[CRM-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-93)** | **Status Page Updates** | While primarily a status page request, it involves syncing **incident updates** (often sourced from PagerDuty) to a public page to reduce duplicate support tickets during outages. |
| **[CRM-17](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-17)** | **Financial Risk Alerting** | To trigger PagerDuty alerts if the **rolling 30-day chargeback rate** exceeds 0.4% (warning) or 0.5% (critical). |

### **Summary of Themes**
*   **Proactive Detection**: Using customer support ticket volume as a "canary" for engineering incidents.
*   **Operational Efficiency**: Reducing manual overhead by syncing incident statuses and auto-resolving tickets when PagerDuty alerts are cleared.
*   **Risk Management**: Monitoring financial thresholds (chargebacks) to ensure immediate response to potential payment processor issues.

Based on an analysis of the 84 medium-priority issues in the **CRM** project, the team is currently addressing several critical gaps in incident management. These gaps range from early detection and alerting to support-engineering collaboration and customer transparency.

### 1. Detection & Alerting Gaps
The team is moving away from reactive support by implementing automated detection mechanisms:
*   **Anomaly Detection**: **[CRM-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-99)** and **[CRM-98](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-98)** focus on building anomaly detection algorithms and monitoring ticket volume spikes to identify outages before they are manually reported.
*   **Alerting Integration**: **[CRM-100](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-100)** and **[CRM-95](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-95)** are bridging the gap between support and engineering by integrating incident detection directly with **PagerDuty**.

### 2. Support-to-Engineering Collaboration
Several issues address the "silo" effect between the support desk and the engineering team:
*   **Automated Bug Reporting**: **[CRM-82](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-82)** and **[CRM-83](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-83)** implement bidirectional sync and automatic Jira bug creation from support tickets.
*   **Incident Linking**: **[CRM-96](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-96)** addresses the lack of organization during major events by building ticket-to-incident linking and duplicate prevention.
*   **Crash Context**: **[CRM-78](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-78)** integrates **Sentry** crash data directly into support tickets to provide engineers with immediate technical context.

### 3. Customer Transparency & Communication
There is a clear effort to improve how customers are informed during service disruptions:
*   **Status Page Implementation**: **[CRM-94](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-94)** and **[CRM-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-93)** involve evaluating and building a "Known Issues" status page with customer subscription capabilities.
*   **Proactive Notifications**: **[CRM-59](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-59)** implements a notification system to update customers on status changes automatically.

### 4. Technical Context & Root Cause Analysis (RCA)
To reduce the "back-and-forth" between agents and customers, the team is automating data collection:
*   **Environment Auto-Detection**: **[CRM-91](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-91)**, **[CRM-90](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-90)**, and **[CRM-89](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-89)** focus on automatically collecting the customer's technical environment (browser, OS, version) at the time of ticket creation.
*   **Error Pattern Matching**: **[CRM-87](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-87)** builds an engine to match error patterns, helping agents identify known bugs faster.

### 5. SLA & Escalation Management
Gaps in meeting service level agreements are being addressed through better visibility:
*   **Real-time Monitoring**: **[CRM-24](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-24)** and **[CRM-23](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-23)** introduce real-time SLA indicators and timer logic for agent queues.
*   **Compliance Reporting**: **[CRM-25](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-25)** automates weekly SLA compliance reporting to identify systemic bottlenecks.

### Summary of Gaps Addressed
| Gap Category | Key Issues | Objective |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Proactive Detection** | **[CRM-99](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-99)**, **[CRM-98](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-98)** | Identify incidents via data trends rather than user reports. |
| **Cross-Team Sync** | **[CRM-83](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-83)**, **[CRM-82](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-82)** | Reduce friction between Support (CRM) and Engineering (Jira). |
| **External Comms** | **[CRM-94](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-94)**, **[CRM-93](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-93)** | Reduce ticket volume during outages via public status updates. |
| **Diagnostic Data** | **[CRM-91](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-91)**, **[CRM-80](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/CRM-80)** | Provide immediate technical context to speed up RCA. |

Based on the current issues in the **BUGS** project, the primary rendering and performance bottlenecks affecting mobile users are detailed in **[BUGS-72](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-72)**.

### Key Rendering & Performance Bottlenecks:

1.  **Main Thread Markdown Rendering**: Rich text and markdown rendering are occurring on the main thread during scrolling, causing significant stutter.
2.  **Synchronous Image Loading**: Avatar images are being loaded synchronously, which blocks the UI thread and prevents smooth scrolling.
3.  **Uncached Layout Calculations**: Cell height calculations for the issue list are computationally expensive and are not currently being cached, leading to redundant processing.
4.  **Inefficient Memory Management**: The application is holding too many reused cells in memory (50 cells for a 100+ item list), which contributes to performance degradation.
5.  **High Crash Rates**: As noted in **[BUGS-51](https://sockcop.atlassian.net/browse/BUGS-51)**, the mobile apps are experiencing crash rates above target (2.3% on iOS and 3.8% on Android), which is being addressed as part of a broader stability initiative.

### Impact:
These bottlenecks have resulted in frame rates dropping to **15-20fps** on mid-range devices and a decline in App Store ratings from 4.5 to 3.9 stars.

Based on a review of recent issues in the **BUGS** and **CRM** projects, the main categories of technical work being prioritized are focused on system reliability, modernization of core workflows, and enhancing the developer/customer experience.

### **1. BUGS: Infrastructure & Core System Hardening**
The BUGS project is currently focused on four major technical initiatives:

*   **Authentication & Security Reliability**: Hardening the OAuth token refresh process, fixing SSO/SAML parsing issues for enterprise customers, and addressing memory leaks in authentication state management.
*   **Search Performance & Relevance**: A major migration from Elasticsearch 6.8 to 8.x is underway, alongside improvements to search relevance scoring (incorporating severity and recency) and the implementation of real-time index updates via Kafka.
*   **Mobile Stability & Performance**: Addressing critical stability issues in iOS and Android apps, specifically crashes related to large attachments, excessive battery drain from background sync, and fixing deep-linking bugs.
*   **API Modernization (v2)**: Transitioning to a more consistent REST v2 architecture and introducing GraphQL support. This includes implementing cursor-based pagination, standardized error handling, and a new webhook system for real-time events.

### **2. CRM: Workflow Automation & Proactive Support**
The CRM project is prioritizing the automation of high-volume support tasks and improving security:

*   **Billing & Dispute Automation**: Streamlining the dispute resolution process by implementing automated evidence retrieval from payment providers (Stripe/Braintree) and building self-service portals for customers.
*   **Account Recovery & Identity Verification**: Modernizing the account recovery flow with SMS-based verification, backup codes, and identity verification integrations (Jumio) to reduce manual support overhead.
*   **Refund Efficiency & Fraud Detection**: Developing an "Instant Refund" eligibility engine and implementing Machine Learning models to detect refund fraud, alongside proactive refund triggers for shipping delays.
*   **Technical Incident Management**: Building tighter integrations between support tickets and engineering incidents, including automated environment detection for bug reports and proactive alerting for ticket volume anomalies.

### **Summary of Prioritization**
| Category | Key Focus Areas |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Reliability** | Auth race conditions, Mobile crashes, Search index lag. |
| **Modernization** | API v2 (REST/GraphQL), Elasticsearch 8.x migration. |
| **Automation** | Billing disputes, Refund eligibility, Account recovery. |
| **Proactive Support** | Shipping delay refunds, Anomaly detection, Status pages. |

There are 4 key issues in the **BUGS** project specifically related to datetime and timezone handling that need to be addressed:
